papers.title: Massive star cluster formation II. Runaway stars as fossils of sub-cluster mergers
papers.author_names: Brooke Polak Mordecai-Mark Mac Low Ralf S. Klessen Simon Portegies Zwart Eric P. Andersson Sabrina M. Appel Claude Cournoyer-Cloutier Simon C. O. Glover Stephen L. W. McMillan

abstract.text: Two main mechanisms have classically been proposed for the formation of runaway stars. In the binary supernova scenario (BSS), a massive star in a binary explodes as a supernova, ejecting its companion. In the dynamical ejection scenario, a star is ejected during a strong dynamical encounter between multiple stars. We propose a third mechanism for the formation of runaway stars: the subcluster ejection scenario (SCES), where a subset of stars from an infalling subcluster is ejected out of the cluster via a tidal interaction with the contracting gravitational potential of the assembling cluster. We demonstrate the SCES in a star-by-star simulation of the formation of a young massive cluster from a $10^6\\\\rm~M_\\\\odot$ gas cloud using the Torch framework. This star cluster forms hierarchically through a sequence of subcluster mergers determined by the initial turbulent, spherical conditions of the gas. We find that these mergers drive the formation of runaway stars in our model. Late-forming subclusters fall into the central potential, where they are tidally disrupted, forming tidal tails of runaway stars that are distributed highly anisotropically. Runaways formed in the same SCES have similar ages, velocities, and ejection directions. Surveying observations, we identify several SCES candidate groups with anisotropic ejection directions. The SCES is capable of producing runaway binaries: two wide dynamical binaries in infalling subclusters were tightened through ejection. This allows for another velocity kick via subsequent via a subsequent BSS ejection. An SCES-BSS ejection is a possible avenue for the creation of hypervelocity stars unbound to the Galaxy. We expect nonspherical initial gas distributions to increase the number of calculated runaway stars. The observation of groups of runaway stars formed via the SCES can thus reveal the assembly history of their natal clusters.

brief.tldr: [research paper] A star-by-star simulation of massive cluster formation reveals a third mechanism for runaway star production—the subcluster ejection scenario (SCES), where tidally disrupted infalling subclusters eject streams of anisotropically distributed runaway stars with similar ages, velocities, and ejection directions. The SCES generates coherent groups of runaways with shared kinematic properties, including the formation of runaway binaries that can undergo secondary ejections via binary supernovae, potentially producing hypervelocity stars. The mechanism is sensitive to initial gas turbulence and subcluster merger dynamics, and its observational signatures—such as anisotropic ejection patterns—can serve as tracers of cluster assembly history.

source_fields.title: Massive star cluster formation II. Runaway stars as fossils of sub-cluster mergers

source_fields.sections_text: Methods: The paper proposes using the TORCH framework to simulate star cluster formation in turbulent molecular clouds by combining adaptive mesh refinement hydrodynamics, sink particle-based star formation, and stellar feedback, with the core idea of modeling gravitational collapse and star formation through self-consistent coupling of gas dynamics, sub-grid star formation, and stellar evolution. Results: The simulation reveals that tidal disruption of infalling subclusters during cluster formation ejects two distinct groups of runaway stars—group 1 (older, slower, ~38.6 km/s) and group 2 (younger, faster, ~87.1 km/s)—with highly anisotropic velocity distributions, low-mass overpopulation, and age-velocity clustering, indicating a mechanism for early runaway star production in young clusters. Footnotes: The section provides footnotes detailing the definitions, origins, and formation mechanisms of runaway and unbound stars, including dynamical binaries, quiescent neutron star companions, and the role of supermassive black hole encounters in producing high-velocity stars. Discussion: Runaway star groups exhibit persistent anisotropy across viewing angles, with strongly peaked escape direction distributions and detectable secondary peaks indicating distinct subcluster ejection histories, enabling reconstruction of cluster assembly timelines through kinematic and age-based grouping, particularly via the coexistence of high-mass O/B stars and predicted large populations of low-mass stars consistent with the initial mass function. Conclusions: The SCES is a previously overlooked channel for runaway star formation, arising from late-forming subclusters that merge and are tidally disrupted, producing anisotropic, velocity-correlated runaways that can serve as observational fossils of cluster assembly history and potentially explain observed runaway binaries and hyper-velocity stars. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel subcluster ejection scenario (SCES) as a third mechanism for producing runaway stars during the tidal disruption of young star clusters, offering a self-consistent, star-by-star simulation-based solution to the long-standing problem of identifying the origin of runaway stars beyond the binary supernova and dynamical ejection models. II. Runaway stars as fossils of subcluster mergers: This section proposes that runaway stars are fossil remnants of past subcluster mergers in star-forming regions, preserving evidence of dynamical interactions within dense stellar environments.

We find that these mergers drive the formation of runaway stars in our model.

title: Runaway stars as fossils of sub-cluster mergers

catalog_assessment.evidence: Purely theoretical/simulation paper proposing a new formation mechanism (SCES) using star-by-star simulations. Mentions 'surveying observations' and 'identify several SCES candidate groups' but does not present new observational data or catalog.

papers.title: The Catalog of early-type Runaway stars from LAMOST-DR8
papers.author_names: Yanjun Guo Luqian Wang Chao Liu You Wu ZhanWen Han XueFei Chen

abstract.text: Runaway stars are OB-type stars ejected from their birthplace with large peculiar velocities. The leading hypothesis addressed in their formation includes the supernova ejection mechanism and the dynamic ejection scenario. Identification of runaway populations is the first step to investigating their formation and evolution. Here we present our work of searching for Galactic runaway candidate stars from the LAMOST Medium-Resolution Survey DR8 database. After studying the kinematic properties for a collection of 4,432 early-type stars, predominantly B-type stars, using the radial velocity measurements from LAMOST DR8 and astrometric solutions made by Gaia DR3, we identified 229 runaway candidate stars. They span a wide distribution in projected rotational velocities. We investigated the Galactic spatial distribution of the runaway population and noticed that most of them likely reside within the Galactic thin disk. Based upon analyzing the Doppler shifts of the candidate stars, we found two binary runaway candidates displaying velocity variation with estimated orbital periods of 40 and 61 days.

brief.tldr: [astronomical survey research paper] A kinematic and spectroscopic survey of 4,432 early-type (B-type) stars from LAMOST DR8 and Gaia DR3 identifies 229 runaway candidate stars with peculiar velocities exceeding 40 km/s, most concentrated in the Galactic thin disk. The study leverages homogeneous radial velocity data to validate kinematic candidates and reveals two binary runaway systems with orbital periods of 40 and 61 days, providing direct evidence for binary dynamics in runaway formation and constraining the binary supernova ejection scenario.

source_fields.title: The Catalog of early-type Runaway stars from LAMOST-DR8

source_fields.sections_text: Summary: This study identifies 229 Galactic runaway OB-type star candidates using kinematic analysis of LAMOST and Gaia data, revealing a predominantly B-type population with diverse rotation and velocity properties, and finds two binary candidate runaways with orbital periods of 40 and 61 days, offering new insights into their formation and origins. Footnotes: The footnotes provide technical definitions and references supporting key concepts in statistical analysis, deep learning, stellar astrophysics, and measurement validity. The Sample: The study uses a sample of 8,011 high-quality early-type stars from the LAMOST MRS, with emission-line stars removed using a CNN-based classifier to ensure accurate radial velocity measurements. Introduction: This paper presents a novel statistical and kinematic analysis of Galactic OB runaway stars, addressing the problem of identifying and validating runaway candidates beyond the binary supernova scenario by leveraging precise astrometric data to improve the reliability and completeness of runaway star catalogs. Properties Of The Runaway Stars: This section identifies and analyzes 229 runaway candidate stars from the LAMOST MRS DR8 sample, assessing their statistical properties and completeness while evaluating their peculiar velocities and spatial distribution. Identification Of Runaway Candidate Stars: This section describes the methodology for identifying runaway candidate stars by transforming stellar coordinates to the Galactic system, calculating their peculiar velocities, and applying kinematic selection criteria based on tangential, radial, and space velocities to distinguish high-velocity stars from the general stellar population.

Here we present our work of searching for Galactic runaway candidate stars from the LAMOST Medium-Resolution Survey DR8 database.

After studying the kinematic properties for a collection of 4,432 early-type stars, predominantly B-type stars, using the radial velocity measurements from LAMOST DR8 and astrometric solutions made by
Gaia DR3, we identified 229 runaway candidate stars.
Based upon analyzing the Doppler shifts of the candidate stars, we found two binary runaway candidates displaying velocity variation with estimated orbital periods of 40 and 61 days.
title: The Catalog of early-type Runaway stars from LAMOST-DR8

catalog_assessment.evidence: Title explicitly states 'Catalog of early-type Runaway stars'. Abstract describes identification of 229 runaway candidate stars from LAMOST DR8 and Gaia DR3 data, providing kinematic analysis and binary detection.

papers.title: Deep HST imaging favors the bulgeless edge-on galaxy explanation for the hypothetical stellar wake created by a runaway supermassive black hole
papers.author_names: Mireia Montes Jorge Sánchez Almeida Ignacio Trujillo

abstract.text: A long linear structure recently discovered could be the stellar wake produced by the passage of a runaway supermassive black hole (SMBH) or, alternatively, a bulgeless edge-on galaxy. We report on new very deep HST imaging that seems to be in tension with the SMBH runaway scenario but is consistent with the bulgeless edge-on galaxy scenario. The new observations were aimed at detecting two key features expected in the SMBH scenario, namely, the bow shock formed where the SMBH meets the surrounding medium, and a counter stellar wake created by another binary SMBH hypothesized as part of the ejection mechanism. Neither of these two features appears to be present in the new images, as would be expected in the edge-on galaxy scenario.

triage.label: rule-weak-llm-confirmed
triage.reason: Weak title match: runaway-related mechanisms LLM confirmed: Paper directly investigates the 'runaway supermassive black hole' scenario as explanation for a stellar wake, which involves ejected/high-velocity objects creating stellar structures.

brief.skipped_reason: weak rule match; retained with search metadata only

source_fields.sections_text: Results: The deep HST imaging reveals the absence of a blue bow shock and counter wake, and an inconsistent color gradient with the stellar wake scenario, supporting the edge-on galaxy interpretation over the runaway SMBH hypothesis, with a rest-frame J-band observation proposed to definitively distinguish between the two. Rationale And Data: New deep HST UV imaging rules out the SMBH runaway scenario by failing to detect predicted bow shocks and counter stellar wakes, supporting instead the alternative explanation of a bulgeless edge-on galaxy.

abstract: A long linear structure recently discovered could be the stellar wake produced by the passage of a runaway supermassive black hole (SMBH) or, alternatively, a bulgeless edge-on galaxy.
We report on new very deep HST imaging that seems to be in tension with the SMBH runaway scenario but is consistent with the bulgeless edge-on galaxy scenario.
title: imaging favors the bulgeless edge-on galaxy explanation for the hypothetical stellar wake created by a runaway

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper presents deep HST imaging to test hypotheses about a specific linear structure. Focus is on interpreting a single feature as either a runaway SMBH wake or edge-on galaxy, not on presenting object-level stellar data or catalogs.
